region_of_origin: Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish adapted for Singapore market)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bánh Mì or plant-based beef bánh mì. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Sandwiches) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for bánh mì or plant-based meat bánh mì in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 (Sandwich) is the closest available parent concept. No specific code for bánh mì or Impossible Beef bánh mì exists.

allergens: soy (tofu, soy sauce, Impossible Beef contains soy protein)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bánh mì sandwich (approximately 220 g) consisting of a Vietnamese-style baguette filled with Impossible Beef, tofu, stir-fried mushrooms, pickled vegetables, cilantro, and chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Impossible Burger (NDB 2346009), Tofu firm (NDB 16126), White bread baguette (NDB 18033), Pickled vegetables, mushrooms, and condiments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for vegetarian bánh mì with Impossible Beef. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the key components: Impossible Burger patty (~30% by weight), firm tofu (~15%), Vietnamese-style baguette (~35%), pickled daikon/carrot (~10%), mushrooms (~5%), condiments/sauce (~5%). Per-100g values derived from this weighted blend. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bread and mixed sandwich meals — analogous estimate based on Vietnamese-style white baguette and mixed plant-based filling; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetarian bánh mì with Impossible Beef. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) white baguette GI ~57 (University of Sydney GI Database), moderated by (2) substantial protein and fibre from Impossible Beef, tofu, and vegetables, which lower the composite meal GI. Mixed meal GI estimated at ~50 (low category, ≤55) consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation principles in Brand-Miller et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 55.9) / 100 = 27.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~924 mg per serving) from soy sauce, chilli sauce, and Impossible Beef seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium load (~924 mg per serving, approximately 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~27.9 per serving) driven by refined white baguette carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fibre from plant-based filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware of the moderate glycemic load per serving (~27.9); portion control is advised, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or choosing a smaller baguette portion can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from Impossible Beef; however, cholesterol is zero and the dish is plant-based
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (~924 mg per serving) is the primary concern; the plant-based protein profile (zero cholesterol, moderate saturated fat at ~1.8 g/100 g) is relatively favourable compared to animal-meat equivalents, but sodium reduction is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Bánh Mì with Impossible Beef is a plant-based adaptation of the classic Vietnamese bánh mì sandwich, served at Co Chung Restaurant in Singapore. The dish features a crispy Vietnamese-style French baguette filled with a savoury mixture of Impossible plant-based ground beef, stir-fried mushrooms, firm tofu, pickled daikon and carrot, fresh cucumber, cilantro, sliced chilli, chilli sauce, and soy sauce. The Impossible Beef provides a meat-like texture and umami flavour while being entirely plant-derived, making this dish suitable for vegetarians. The baguette is light and crispy on the outside with a soft interior, and the filling delivers a complex balance of savoury, tangy, spicy, and herbaceous flavours characteristic of bánh mì. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 198 kcal per 100 g, with 9.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 25.4 g carbohydrate. The protein content is notable for a vegetarian sandwich, contributed by Impossible Beef and tofu. Dietary fibre is approximately 2.8 g per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at ~420 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoned plant-based meat, making this a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low category), moderated by the protein and fibre content of the plant-based filling. Cholesterol is zero. This dish is relevant for vegetarians seeking high-protein plant-based meals in a Singapore hawker or restaurant context.
common_user_queries: What is Impossible Beef bánh mì?
Calories in vegetarian bánh mì Singapore
Is Impossible Beef bánh mì vegan?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for vegetarian bánh mì with Impossible Beef exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries (Impossible Burger, firm tofu, white baguette, pickled vegetables, mushrooms, condiments). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for white baguette and mixed-meal GI attenuation principles. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish exists. Values should be verified against laboratory analysis if used for clinical or regulatory purposes. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation: this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; Impossible Beef does not contain added B12 in all formulations — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Impossible Burger entry NDB 2346009). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero: no vitamin D-containing ingredients in this plant-based dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component analysis). 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central Impossible Burger and tofu entries). 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero: all ingredients are plant-based with no animal-derived cholesterol). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 sentinel not used; value estimated at 2.1 g from USDA component blend. 7. GI estimated at 50 (low, ≤55) using analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white baguette GI ~57 moderated by mixed-meal protein and fibre attenuation. 8. Serving size estimated at 220 g based on typical bánh mì sandwich weight. 9. is_vegan set to false because Impossible Beef may contain trace animal-derived processing aids depending on formulation; dish is confirmed vegetarian. 10. Sodium is high (~420 mg/100 g); clinical caution advised for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. 11. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA composite estimation for this mixed dish and are documented as not individually resolvable from the component blend used.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full composite nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central component entries (Impossible Burger, firm tofu, white baguette, pickled vegetables, mushrooms, condiments).
Estimated GI at 50 using University of Sydney GI Database white baguette reference moderated by mixed-meal protein/fibre attenuation.
Added all five mandatory Singaporean-cuisine local-language names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as origin language.
Documented all zero values (vitamin_b12, vitamin_d, trans_fat, cholesterol) with confirmed-zero justifications in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values (closest_parent) for all three ontology systems.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI 50 ≤ 55 threshold rule.
Included Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name field correctly set.
